title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
statement: Given a string `s`, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters. A substring must be contiguous — you can't skip characters.
constraints: s consists of English letters, digits, symbols, and spaces
compare_fn:
def _compare(result, expected, args):
    return result == expected

test_cases.explanation_if_failed: 'a' appears at index 0 and again at index 3, but by the time we see the second 'a', the window has already moved past index 0. A solution that jumps `left` to `last_seen[ch] + 1` without checking that `last_seen[ch]` is still inside the current window will incorrectly shrink the window and under-count the answer.

The constraint allows spaces in `s`, and 'a' and 'A' are different characters — a solution that strips/normalizes whitespace, or that lowercases characters before comparing them, will miscount this. The longest run is 'A a' (indices 2-4), length 3.

brute_force.description: Check every possible substring (O(n^2) of them), and for each one scan it to verify all characters are unique (O(n)).
brute_force.complexity: O(n^3) time (or O(n^2) with a smarter uniqueness check)
brute_force.why_insufficient: At n = 5*10^4, even O(n^2) is 2.5 billion operations — this needs to run in roughly linear time.

optimal_approach.description: Sliding window with two pointers: expand the right pointer each step; keep a map of each character's last-seen index; when a repeat is found inside the current window, jump the left pointer just past that repeat's previous position.
optimal_approach.complexity: O(n) time, O(min(n, charset size)) space

hint_ladder.text: Instead of checking every substring from scratch, can you extend a window one character at a time and only shrink it when you hit a repeat?
Keep a map from character to the last index where you saw it.
When the current character is already in your map, move the left edge of your window to just after that character's last occurrence.
Watch out: only jump the left pointer forward if that last-seen index is actually still inside the current window — otherwise you can move left backward by mistake.

common_loopholes.description: A string of all-identical characters should return 1, not 0.
A character seen before but now outside the current window must not incorrectly shrink it.
Spaces are valid characters in the input, and letter casing matters ('a' != 'A') — don't strip whitespace or normalize case.

key_points: Answer is a LENGTH, not the substring itself
'Without repeating characters' means every character in the window is unique
Empty string is a valid input (answer 0)

complexity_probe.arg_generator:
import random
def _generate_complexity_args(n):
    # Bounded, realistic alphabet (matches the problem's own
    # constraint) -- by the pigeonhole principle this caps a
    # window-based approach's per-position work at the alphabet
    # size regardless of n, which is exactly what makes the
    # authored O(n) sliding window correct and distinguishes it
    # from a from-scratch, no-reuse check-every-substring brute
    # force (which stays slow regardless of alphabet size).
    random.seed(3579 + n)
    alphabet = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz'
    return {'s': ''.join(random.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(n))}
